figures.gloss: The living, divinely designated Imam in unbroken descent from 'Ali and Fatima through Isma'il — for Nizaris today the Aga Khan (the 50th Imam, since 2025); for Tayyibis an Imam in concealment represented by the Da'i al-Mutlaq of the Bohra communities.
figures.facet: Shi'ism's boldest continuity claim kept literal: where Twelvers await a hidden Imam, Nizari Ismailism maintains a PRESENT one — revelation's interpreter alive, addressable, and issuing guidance now.
figures.provenance: Fatimid imamate doctrine (al-Qadi al-Nu'man, Da'a'im al-Islam); Farhad Daftary, The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines; the Aga Khan succession of 2025 is public record.

figures.gloss: Every revelation has an outward letter (zahir) and an inner truth (batin) reachable only through the Imam's authorized interpretation (ta'wil) — the hermeneutic that earned Ismailis the polemical name Batiniyya.
figures.facet: Esotericism as constitutional principle rather than mood: the inner meaning is not free for the finding but keyed to a living interpretive office — hermeneutics and imamate lock into a single doctrine.
figures.provenance: Fatimid ta'wil literature (al-Qadi al-Nu'man, Ja'far ibn Mansur al-Yaman); analyzed in Daftary and in Henry Corbin's studies of Ismaili gnosis.

figures.gloss: The graded missionary organization — da'is, hujjas, and ranks of instruction — that carried Ismailism from clandestine cells across the Abbasid world to an empire: the Fatimid caliphate (909-1171) that built Cairo and al-Azhar.
figures.facet: The rare case of an esoteric hierarchy scaling into a state: the same initiatory ladder that swore in a village disciple administered an empire from Sicily to Sind at its height.
figures.provenance: Fatimid da'wa documented in al-Qadi al-Nu'man and later Tayyibi sources; Daftary, The Isma'ilis; Heinz Halm, The Empire of the Mahdi.

figures.gloss: The Elburz mountain fortress seized by Hasan-i Sabbah in 1090, seat of the Nizari state and its famed library until the Mongols razed it in 1256 — and the site where the Qiyama, the spiritual Resurrection, was proclaimed in 1164.
figures.facet: The most defamed address in religious history: the 'Assassins' of Crusader rumor and Marco Polo's drugged-garden tale were a besieged scholastic community whose real story — hostile sources burned their own — had to be reconstructed from their enemies; the legend, not the order, is the sensation.
figures.provenance: Juwayni's hostile eyewitness account of Alamut's fall; the legend dismantled in Farhad Daftary, The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis; 'hashishiyya' attested as a pejorative label, not a description of practice.

figures.gloss: The anonymous 10th-century Basran encyclopedia — 52 epistles running from arithmetic and music to cosmology and the soul's ascent — framing all the sciences as one ladder back to the One.
figures.facet: Esoteric Islam's bid to own the whole curriculum: number, harmony, and Neoplatonic emanation braided into a single initiatory syllabus — its precise Ismaili affiliation is a long scholarly debate (adopted by the later Tayyibi da'wa, whatever its origin), flagged.
figures.provenance: The Rasa'il themselves (Oxford/IIS critical edition in progress since 2008); affiliation debate surveyed in Daftary and in Nader El-Bizri's introductory volume.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: The Sevener imamate descends directly from the Shi'i doctrine of divinely designated leadership, branching at Ja'far al-Sadiq's succession in 765 — proven doctrinal descent within Islam.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze faith crystallized in 1017 inside the Fatimid Ismaili da'wa around the caliph-imam al-Hakim — documented parent-child descent; the child then sealed itself off from the parent.
GOLD SEAM: Hamza ibn 'Ali built Druze doctrine with the tools of Fatimid ta'wil — the batin/zahir hermeneutic carried out of Ismailism into the new faith, then radicalized.
GOLD SEAM: The Epistles pour Plotinian emanation into Islamic form via the Arabic Plotinus corpus (the so-called Theology of Aristotle) — documented textual dependence, not mere resemblance.
GOLD SEAM: The Druze division into initiated 'uqqal and uninitiated juhhal carries forward the Ismaili grading of access to the batin — the esoteric-tier structure inherited from the parent da'wa.

dossier.essence: The second-largest branch of Shi'i Islam, tracing the imamate through Isma'il, son of Ja'far al-Sadiq (d. 765), and once sovereign as the Fatimid caliphate (909-1171) that founded Cairo and al-Azhar. Nizari Ismailis worldwide follow a living, present Imam — the Aga Khan — while the Tayyibi branch (the Bohra communities of South Asia and Yemen) holds its Imam in concealment, represented by the Da'i al-Mutlaq.
dossier.cosmos: Ismaili thought weds Qur'anic revelation to Neoplatonic emanation: creation proceeds from God through Intellect and Soul, and sacred history unfolds in prophetic cycles, each law-giving revelation bearing an outward letter (zahir) and an inner truth (batin). Salvation is the soul's ascent through knowledge of that inner meaning, whose authorized key is the divinely designated Imam of the age; the resurrection is read spiritually — most radically in the Nizari proclamation of the Qiyama at Alamut in 1164.
dossier.practice: Devotional life is inflected throughout by the imamate: allegiance and tithes to the Imam or his representative, graded religious instruction descending from the old missionary hierarchy (the da'wa) that once carried the faith from clandestine cells to an empire, and esoteric interpretation (ta'wil) as a discipline of study. Nizari communities gather in jamatkhanas under the Aga Khan's guidance, paired today with a large development network; Tayyibi Bohras maintain Fatimid-derived law and liturgy under the Da'i al-Mutlaq.
dossier.canon: The Qur'an, read through a distinctive Fatimid corpus — al-Qadi al-Nu'man's Da'a'im al-Islam and the ta'wil literature — together with the Rasa'il Ikhwan al-Safa adopted by the Tayyibi da'wa and, for Nizaris, the Alamut-era and later Persian tradition.
dossier.reflects: Esotericism made constitutional — the conviction that revelation carries a hidden meaning whose key is not free for the finding but vested in a living, divinely designated interpreter.
sources: Farhad Daftary, 'The Isma'ilis: Their History and Doctrines' (1990)
Heinz Halm, 'The Empire of the Mahdi: The Rise of the Fatimids' (1996)
Farhad Daftary, 'The Assassin Legends: Myths of the Isma'ilis' (1994)
